text: In this video, we present the case of a seventy-three-year-old woman who presented to our institution with signs and symptoms of acute small bowel obstruction.
She has an extensive past medical history, including schizophrenia, for which she lives in a group home.
The patient underwent CT imaging.
As is visible on the scout image, there is evidence of dilated small bowel loops and perhaps air within the gallbladder, marked by the circle.
On axial imaging, there is evidence of large calcified gallstones within the gallbladder as well as periduodenal inflammation with evidence of a fistulus tract from the gallbladder to the duodenum.
The video shows a gallstone located within the small intestine in the pelvis, visibly causing an obstruction.
These findings are re-demonstrated on the coronal reconstructions.
We suspected a cholecystoduodenal fistula as well as a small bowel obstruction from a gallstone within the intestinal tract consistent with gallstone ileus.
Traditionally, gallstone ileus has two treatment goals.
The first and most important being removal of the obstructing gallstone within the small intestine.
In select patients, an attempt at cholecystectomy with cholecystoduodenal fistula division may be attempted.
However, as most patients present moribund, the additional time and added risk may not be warranted.
In an effort to utilize the benefits of minimally invasive surgery, these patients may be dealt with in a laparoscopic manner.
We present a novel option that, in the setting of patients who have large residual stones within the gallbladder lumen,
A laparoscopic cholecystotomy with extraction of these stones and gallbladder closure may be utilized as a safe alternative for cholecystectomy.
The surgeon elevates the greater omentum to expose the small bowel, revealing the anatomical structures necessary for the small bowel resection procedure.
The area of obstruction from the gallstone was clearly evident.
The surgeon places sutures and performs a longitudinal enterotomy on the small bowel, carefully incising the tissue to access the bowel lumen.
The surgeon retrieves the stone and closes the enterotomy in a transverse fashion to prevent stricturing of the small bowel.
The surgeon inspects the remainder of the small intestine, visually examining the bowel to ensure no additional large stones are present.
The surgical team shifts focus to the right upper quadrant, preparing to address anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon observes an extensive inflammatory response surrounding the gallbladder, with visible signs of inflammation in the tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the surrounding omentum, revealing the fundus of the gallbladder.
The surgeon performs a cholecystotomy, making an incision into the gallbladder to address the presence of stones or inflammation.
The surgeon retrieves gallstones from the lumen of the gallbladder, carefully extracting them from the inflamed tissue.
The cholecystoduodenal fistula becomes clearly visible after completing the previous surgical steps.
The surgical field shows the gallbladder with no residual stones visible within its lumen.
The surgeon irrigates the right upper quadrant and closes the gallbladder using several interrupted figure-of-eight silk sutures.
The surgeon irrigates the upper abdomen and places a drain near the gallbladder, ensuring proper fluid management and drainage in the surgical area.
The patient remained on antibiotics postoperatively and was discharged from the hospital within six days.
To our knowledge, this is the first report of performing a cholecystotomy with stone retrieval and gallbladder closure in the setting of gallstone ileus.
We believe this may be a viable option in select patients as it allows for minimally invasive techniques and avoids the added risk of bile duct injury or duodenal fistula.
There is also theoretical risk of decreasing the possibility of recurrent small bowel obstructions, although this warrants further investigation.
